All solutions

Law Firm Web Design

Law firm websites built to turn a search into a signed client

Someone searching for an attorney is usually having a bad week. They are anxious, comparing three firms in three tabs, and deciding in seconds whether yours looks like it can handle their problem. Everything below is built around that moment.

Start a project See the designs

14

Years building software

6

Design concepts below

1

Developer you talk to

Design concepts

Six directions, six kinds of firm

Original concepts built with the studio's design system — not stock templates. Each targets a different practice area and a different kind of client.

These are design concepts, not past client work — the firms shown are fictional. Real engagements start from your name, your practice areas, and your market.

Practice areas

Every practice area gets its own page

Nobody searches for "law firm." They search for the thing that happened to them. Each of these becomes a real page, written and structured to be found.

Personal InjuryCriminal DefenseFamily Law & DivorceEstate Planning & ProbateBusiness & CorporateImmigrationEmployment & LaborReal EstateBankruptcyIntellectual PropertyMedical MalpracticeWorkers' Compensation

What makes it different

A legal site is not a business site with a gavel on it

The visitor is in crisis

They are not browsing. Injury, arrest, divorce, a death in the family — the page has to be calm, obvious, and fast, with the next step never more than one tap away.

Bar rules are not optional

Advertising rules, testimonial restrictions, and prior-results disclaimers vary by state. I build to your jurisdiction's rules and hand you the language for your compliance review.

Practice area is the search unit

Nobody searches for 'law firm.' They search 'car accident lawyer Greenville.' The site is structured as one strong page per practice area and market, not one page about everything.

Intake is the conversion

A form that lands in an unwatched inbox is a lost client. Submissions route to the right person, with the confidentiality notice the form legally needs to carry.

What ships

Practice-area architecture

One page per practice area, per location — the unit people actually search for.

Attorney profiles

Bios, bar admissions, education, and published work, all structured for search.

Case results module

Verdicts and settlements with the disclaimers your state requires.

Intake that routes

Forms and click-to-call wired to your case management or inbox, not a black hole.

Compliance handled

Disclaimers, no-guarantee language, and prior-results notices reviewed against your bar rules.

Accessibility

WCAG 2.1 AA — a real risk area for public-facing legal sites.

Structured data

LegalService, Attorney, and FAQ schema so search engines can read the firm.

Speed & mobile

Core Web Vitals green on a phone, on cell service, which is where the calls come from.

Found, not just built

  • Practice area × location pages

    The page architecture that captures how legal searches are actually typed.

  • LegalService & Attorney schema

    Structured data for the firm, each attorney, and each practice area.

  • Local presence

    Google Business Profile, consistent citations, and location pages for each office.

  • Answer-first content

    The questions clients ask before hiring — written to be found, and to be useful.

  • Migration without loss

    Redesigns ship with a redirect map so existing rankings survive the launch.

  • Reviews within the rules

    A review strategy that respects the testimonial restrictions in your state.

FAQ

Questions firms actually ask

How much does a law firm website cost?

Scope drives it. A focused site for a solo practice is a very different build than a multi-office firm with twelve practice areas and case-management integration. You get a fixed number after a short scoping call — not an hourly meter, and not a number I made up before understanding the firm.

How long does it take?

A focused practice site typically runs 3–5 weeks from kickoff; a larger multi-practice build usually runs 8–12. You see working pages every week rather than waiting for a single reveal at the end.

Do you handle bar compliance and disclaimers?

I build to your state's advertising rules and include the standard disclaimers, prior-results notices, and no-attorney-client-relationship language in the right places. I am a developer, not your ethics counsel — the final language goes to you or your compliance reviewer before launch.

Can you integrate with our case management software?

Yes. Intake forms can route into Clio, MyCase, Filevine, Smokeball, or plain email and your CRM. If the tool has an API or a webhook, it can be wired up.

Do you do the SEO too, or just the site?

The site ships SEO-ready: practice-area architecture, structured data, clean technical foundation, and a content plan for the pages that will earn traffic next. Ongoing content and link work is a separate engagement if you want it.

We already have a site. Can you redesign it without losing our rankings?

Yes, and that planning happens before anything is built. Existing URLs get mapped and redirected, high-performing pages are identified and preserved, and rankings are watched through the launch window.

Who owns the website when it's done?

You do — the code, the content, the domain, and the accounts. No proprietary platform you can only leave by starting over.

Do you work with solo attorneys and small firms?

Yes. A solo practice with one sharp practice-area page often outperforms a regional firm with a neglected site. The engagement scales to the firm.

Let's talk about your firm

Tell me your practice areas and the markets you want to win. You'll get an honest read on what your site should be doing differently — and a fixed number to do it.
